text:
2016 BCSC 224 Kent v. Translink
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF BRITISH COLUMBIA
Citation:
Kent v. Translink,
2016 BCSC 224
Date: 20160212
Docket: S155086
Registry: Vancouver
Between:
Stephen Kent

And
TRANSLINK, COAST MOUNTAIN BUS COMPANY, UNIFOR UNION 111, WEST VAN MUNICIPAL TRANSIT MANAGEMENT (CONTRACTOR TO TRANSLINK), GARETH ROWLANDS, MICHAEL KENNY, PETER MORRIS, WEST VAN MUNICIPAL HALL STAFFER / HR ADVISOR, CLARE POLLOCK, AMALGAMATED TRANSIT UNION 134 PRESIDENT, GEOFFREY DEVLIN, CALVIN STEWART, ANDYTAKHAR, WEST VAN TRANSIT TRAINERS, SCOTT BOYLAN, NARESH SACHITHANANDAN, ANDY TAKHAR,

CAMERON MORTON, SARA MYER, BRIAN McNAUGHTON, LYDIA UNKNOWN, ROMAN UNKNOWN, WEST VAN TRANSIT MAINTENANCE DEPARTMENT, TOM CHEUNG, DAVE JAMES, JOHN UNKNOWN, WORK SAFE BC / OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH & SAFETY, ANNE JOPLING, WEST VAN TRANSIT SECRET SHOPPERS, SIMON UNKNOWN, JOHN DOE (BALD IRANIAN), JOHN DOE (ALIAS DAVIE ST GYM HARASSER), JANE DOE (ALIAS BUSY BEE), JOHN DOE (ALIAS CANADA POST HARASSER), JOHN DOE (ALIAS STARBUCKS HARASSER), JOHN DOE (ALIAS SURGICAL MASK GUY), CONCERT REALTY SERVICE, PETER LEE, GREGG UNKNOWN, CECILIA MA, JOHN & JANE DOE (ALIAS Mr & Mrs NEIGHBOUR), JANE DOE (ALIAS Ms NEIGHBOUR # 3), AQUARIUS MEDICAL, JUDITH BOYCE, VINCE TONG, EMMA GALLOWAY, SANJA AVINASHI, Dr GORDON,
WEST VAN POLICE DEPARTMENT, VANCOUVER POLICE DEPARTMENT, URBAN FARE (YALETOWN), JOHN DOE MANAGER, SEAN UNKNOWN STAFF , STEVE NASH FITNESS WORLD (NORTH SHORE), NATALIE UNKNOWN, KEN DOE (EX - SUPERINTENDENT), SCOTT DOE (ALIAS UNDERCOVER SCOTT), JONN DOE (ALIAS G35 GUY), TERRY DOE (ALIAS TRIGGER), JOHN DOE (ALIAS R & W HOOPED SHIRT GUY) , JOHN DOE (ALIAS SLIM SHADY),

Reasons for Judgment

Counsel for the Defendants, Gareth Rowlands, Michael Kenny, Peter Morris, Clare Pollock, Scott Boylan, Naresh Sachithanandan, Andy Takhar, Shawne McCorkell, Karen Krupa, Peter Cockroft, Daniel Bellanger, Alan Donaldson, Robert Bird, Bruce Steel, Kal Kang, Sam Chandi, Cameron Morton, Sara Myer, Brian McNaughton, Tom Cheung and Dave James:

Place and Dates of Hearing:
Vancouver, B.C.
December 15 - 16, 2015 and February 12, 2016
Place and Date of Judgment:
Vancouver, B.C.
February 12, 2016
I. introduction
[1] Before the Court are applications to have the plaintiff's Notice of Civil Claim struck under Rule 9-5 of the Supreme Court Civil Rules.
[2] The various applications serve to include all of the identifiable defendants.
[3] The applications first came before the Court on December 15 and 16, 2015. Submissions by the applicants were made and Mr. Kent, who was self-represented, responded.
[4] At the conclusion of argument, the Court adjourned matters until today in order to provide Mr. Kent with the opportunity to prepare a redraft of his Notice of Civil Claim.
[5] Mr. Kent has prepared two redrafts of his Notice of Civil Claim. The second redraft modifies his first redraft. The redrafts provide no further assistance.
[6] In his Notice of Civil Claim, Mr. Kent seeks significant damages, including punitive damages, and makes serious allegations, including a general conspiracy to intentionally harass him.
II. The LAW
[7] Rule 9-5(1) reads:
At any stage of a proceeding, the court may order to be struck out or amended the whole or any part of a pleading, petition or other document on the ground that
(a) it discloses no reasonable claim or defence, as the case may be,
(b) it is unnecessary, scandalous, frivolous or vexatious,
(c) it may prejudice, embarrass or delay the fair trial or hearing of the proceeding, or
(d) it is otherwise an abuse of the process of the court,
and the court may pronounce judgment or order the proceeding to be stayed or dismissed and may order the costs of the application to be paid as special costs.
[8] In Mohebbi v. North Vancouver RCMP, 2015 BCSC 2083, Justice Williams cites with approval the summary of the jurisprudence on Rule 9-5 by Justice Garson (as she then was) in Dempsey et al v. Envision Credit Union et al., 2006 BCSC 750 which reads:
[13] In summary, a pleading will be struck out if:
(a) the pleadings are unintelligible, confusing and difficult to understand (Citizens of Foreign Aid Reform [Inc. v. Canadian Jewish Congress, 1999 CarswellBC 2111 (S.C.)]);
(b) the pleadings do not establish a cause of action and do not advance a claim known in law (Citizens for Foreign Aid Reform, supra);
(c) the pleadings are without substance in that they are groundless, fanciful and trifle with the Court's time (Borsato v. Basra, [2000 BCSC 28, rev'd on other grounds 2000 BCSC 1898])
(d) the pleadings are not bona fides, are oppressive and are designed to cause the Defendants anxiety, trouble and expense (Borsato v. Basra, supra);
(e) the action is brought for an improper purpose, particularly the harassment and oppression of the Defendants (Ebrahim v. Ebrahim, 2002 BCSC 466).
[9] In Willow v. Chong, 2013 BCSC 1083, Justice Fisher states:
[18] The test for striking a claim as disclosing no reasonable claim under Rule 9-5(1)(a), set out in Hunt v Carey Canada Inc., [1990] 2 SCR 959 and reiterated more recently in R. v Imperial Tobacco Canada Ltd., 2011 SCC 42, is whether it is "plain and obvious," assuming the facts pleaded are true, that the claim discloses no reasonable cause of action, has no reasonable prospect of success, or if the action is "certain to fail." If there is a chance that the plaintiffs might succeed, then they should not be "driven from the judgment seat." No evidence is admissible on an application under Rule 9-5(1)(a).
[19] The rule that material facts in a notice of civil claim must be taken as true does not mean that allegations based on assumption and speculation must be taken as true. This was discussed in Operation Dismantle Inc. v The Queen, [1985] 1 SCR 441, where Dickson J. (as he then was) stated that "[n]o violence is done to the rule where allegations, incapable of proof, are not taken as proven". In Young v Borzoni, 2007 BCCA 16, the court stated (at paras. 30-31) that great caution must be taken in relying on Operation Dismantle as a general authority that allegations in pleadings should be weighed as to their truth, but it is not fundamentally wrong to look behind allegations in some cases, and it may be appropriate to subject the allegations in the pleadings to a sceptical analysis. It was considered appropriate in Young, where the plaintiff made sweeping allegations of things like intolerance, deceit, harassment, intimidation and falsifying documents against the defendants, which the court concluded could only be viewed as speculation.
[20] Under Rule 9-5(1)(b), a pleading is unnecessary or vexatious if it does not go to establishing the plaintiff's cause of action, if it does not advance any claim known in law, where it is obvious that an action cannot succeed, or where it would serve no useful purpose and would be a waste of the court's time and public resources: Citizens for Foreign Aid Reform Inc. v Canadian Jewish Congress, [1999] BCJ No. 2160 (SC); Skender v Farley, 2007 BCCA 629. If a pleading is so confusing that it is difficult to understand what is pleaded, it may also be unnecessary, frivolous or vexatious. An application under this sub-rule may be supported by evidence.
[21] Abuse of process under Rule 9-5(1)(d) or the court's inherent discretion is a flexible doctrine. It allows the court to prevent a claim from proceeding where to do so would violate principles of judicial economy, consistency, finality and the integrity of the administration of justice. A claim may be struck where it is a collateral attack on an administrative decision that is subject to appeal or judicial review: Cimaco International Sales Inc. v British Columbia, 2010 BCCA 342; Stephen v HMTQ, 2008 BCSC 1656; Varzeliotis v British Columbia, 2007 BCSC 620; Gemex Developments Corp. v City of Coquitlam, 2002 BCSC 412; Berscheid v Ensign, [1999] BCJ No. 1172 (SC). A claim may also be struck as an abuse of process where it is an attempt to re-litigate an issue that has already been decided: Toronto (City) v Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE), Local 79, 2003 SCC 63.
[10] In Simon v. Canada (Attorney General), 2015 BCSC 924, in considering an application to strike a notice of civil claim, Justice Donegan states:
[57] First, the pleadings fail to satisfy their basic purpose. The issues are not defined. There is no concise statement of material facts that are necessary to support any complete cause of action. Instead of pleading material facts, the plaintiffs have filed a lengthy, rambling, at some points bizarre narrative filled with irrelevant information, sweeping allegations against named and unnamed persons based upon assumptions and speculation, with scattered references to legislation bearing no relevance to the remedies that are sought.
[58] Second, a number of potential causes of action are pleaded that are unknown to the law. They are not, in my view, the type of novel but arguable claims as described in the case law such that they should be allowed to proceed to trial.
[59] Third, insofar as I can identify any potential causes of action known to law against either or both of the defendants, they appear to include a claim in contract, an overall claim in tort, and a claim based upon unjust enrichment. The tort claim involves a complex combination of various "torts", including perhaps, negligence, breach of statutory duty, misfeasance of public office, and conspiracy. I will address all of these, but I will say that overall my conclusions about all of the plaintiffs' claims are similar to those found by Associate Chief Justice Cullen in Fowler v. Canada (Attorney General), 2012 BCSC 367. I find that the plaintiffs have failed to plead material facts in support of each element of these claims. Any of the claims raised rely upon the plaintiffs' lengthy and argumentative narrative in which it is impossible to separate the material from the immaterial, the fabric of one potential cause of action or claim from that of another, or conjecture and opinion from the asserted facts: Fowler at para. 54. In my view, none of these claims disclose any reasonable cause of action, specifically a contract claim.
[11] In Olenga v. British Columbia, 2015 BCSC 1050, Justice Baird in addressing pleadings alleging conspiracy states:
[21] Pleadings alleging conspiracy must be as specific as possible. The statement of claim should describe who the several parties to the conspiracy are, and their relationship with each other. It should allege the object of the unlawful conspiracy between the defendants, giving the best particulars it can of the dates when or dates between which the unlawful conspiracy was entered into or continued. It should state precisely the objects and means of the alleged conspiracy and the overt acts which are alleged to have been done by each of the alleged conspirators in furtherance of it. It should detail the precise nature of the injury and damage occasioned to the plaintiff: Can-Dive Services Ltd v. Pacific Coast Energy Corp. 1993 B.C.J. No. 2958 at paragraphs 8-9. The sweeping, superficial allegations of conspiracy in the present pleadings fail comprehensively to meet these requirements. They do not come close to achieving the level of specificity, clarity or asserted factual content required by the Rules of Court.
[12] The Notice of Civil Claim and the redrafts make serious allegations going to the reputations of the defendants. In this regard, although dishonesty is not pleaded, the reasons of Justice Donald writing for our Court of Appeal in The Owners, Strata Plan LMS3259 v. Sze Hang Holding Inc., 2015 BCCA 424 apply:
[11] It remains only to say this. Self-represented litigants have no licence to employ accusations of dishonesty as a feature of their rhetoric unless such accusations are firmly grounded in the evidence, are relevant to the proceedings, and are responsibly made. By "responsibly", I mean measured, careful, and faithful to the evidence. Self-represented litigants must understand that the court is not a free fire zone where anything can be said regardless of the harm to others and their reputations.
III. analysis
[13] In the case at bar, the Notice of Civil Claim is prolix, convoluted and very difficult to comprehend. The redrafts are similar in measure. If the Court were not to strike the plaintiff's claim, the defendants would be forced to try to distill order from the abstract. Such a burden would be unfair to each of them.
[14] As Justice Donegan states, pleadings must contain defined issues with a concise statement of the material facts necessary to support a cause of action. In this case at bar, "the pleading fail to satisfy their basic purpose".
[15] A defendant is entitled to know the case to be met, especially where significant damages are sought and serious allegations are made.
[16] The Court strikes, without leave to amend, the plaintiff's Notice of Civil Claim.
[17] The applicants are awarded their respective costs at Scale B.
"Funt J."
